questions.explanation: Dynamic method dispatch calls the overridden method based on the actual object type (B), not the reference type (A). This is runtime polymorphism.

questions.question: Which is an example of compile-time polymorphism?
options: Method overloading with different parameter types
Upcasting a subclass to its parent type
questions.explanation: Method overloading is resolved at compile time based on the method signature. The compiler determines which overloaded version to call.

options: The cast succeeds with default values
questions.explanation: The compiler allows the cast (both are Shape subtypes), but at runtime the JVM sees the actual object is a Circle, not a Rectangle, and throws ClassCastException.

questions.question: An overriding method can have which return type compared to the parent method?
options: The same type or a subtype (covariant return)
Any type — return types don't matter for overriding
The same type or a supertype
questions.explanation: Java supports covariant return types: an overriding method can return a subtype of the declared return type. For example, overriding a method returning Animal can return Dog.

questions.question: What does the `@Override` annotation do?
options: Makes the method run faster at runtime
Causes a compile error if the method doesn't actually override a superclass method
Prevents the method from being overridden further
questions.explanation: @Override is a compile-time check. If the annotated method doesn't actually override (e.g., wrong signature), the compiler reports an error, catching subtle bugs.
